title: requests — regulatory compliance
description: Compliance controls this module produces evidence for. Access requests are authorization decisions — SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria CC6 (logical access) is the primary applicable regime, with GDPR Art. 32 (security of processing) applying to the audit trail itself.

controls.evidence: Every access request produces an approval_instance carrying the full authorization decision trail: who requested, when, for what resource, what permissions, who resolved as approver, who decided, what comment. The `access-requests:audit-report` command produces monthly compliance evidence packages.

Every approve/reject decision goes through the standard `auth:sanctum` middleware. The approver's User row is FK-linked in `approval_decisions.approver_id` and immutable. Impersonation-during-decision is prevented by the platform-plane / tenant-plane guard split.

Every grant materialised via an access request carries `source='access_request'` + `source_reference=<approval_instance_id>`. Auditors can trace ANY entry in access_grants back to (a) an approval_instance with (b) a resolved approver group + (c) at least one approve decision — or reject if the grant reached materialisation through a template.on_timeout='approve' escalation.

Grants sourced from access requests carry `expires_at` (default 7 days from `config('access.requests.default_grant_ttl_days')`). Automated revocation runs via `access/grants::ExpireGrantsJob` at the TTL boundary. Manual revocation also fires `access/grants::GrantRevoked` — our CleanupOrphanedRequestOnGrantRevocationListener writes an audit-trail note back to the originating approval_instance.
health.json + metrics.json + `access-requests:audit-report` weekly output. Orphaned-approval metric alerts on-call when materialisation stalls.
feature-flags.json — every material change ships behind a Pennant flag. Kill-switch on `access_requests.kill_switch` lets ops disable the submit path without a redeploy.
The access-request flow IS the platform's user-facing enforcement of the access-control policy. Every 403 with a `request_url` hint is a policy touchpoint captured in metrics.

The `access-requests:audit-report` output is the input to the quarterly access-rights review. Approvers who approve at outlier rates (very high or very low compared to peers) are surfaced for HR / management review.

Processing of requester + approver identity is necessary for the legitimate interest of operating the authorization loop. No consent basis is needed — access-request activity is directly tied to the contractual context of platform use.

Access-request activity for a given user (as requester OR approver OR resource owner) exports via the compliance module's DSAR orchestrator. Includes the request context, the approvers' decisions with comments, and the resulting grant id.
Erasure on a user's record anonymises their references in approval_instances.context_json + approval_decisions.comment + access_grants.subject_id / granted_by. See retention.json.

Every decision is captured in approval_decisions rows with FK to the approver's User (immutable). Every state transition writes an audit-trail entry — tampering is detectable via the audit-log integrity chain (owned by compliance module).
The 403 body with `request_url` hint is an error-identification touchpoint. The SPA renders a clear 'you don't have permission — request access here' affordance.
The SDUI create screen renders a labelled TextArea for `reason` + a clear submit button. Field descriptions in SDUI accompany every input.
Every state transition fires a broadcast to the requester + approvers via the private channels; SPA surfaces them as toast + inbox updates. Live status is available without polling.
